topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Polynomials > Chebyshev polynomials
prompt: Find a polynomial $x^3 + a x^2 + b x + c$ for which $\max_{x \in [-1, 1]} |x^3 + a x^2 + b x + c|$ is minimised. You may assume that such a polynomial exists.
response_text:
Let
\[
p(x)=x^3+ax^2+bx+c.
\]
We want to choose \(a,b,c\) so that
\[
\max_{x\in[-1,1]} |p(x)|
\]
is as small as possible.

This is a minimax problem, and for monic polynomials on \([-1,1]\), the extremal polynomial is the suitably scaled Chebyshev polynomial.

## 1. Use the Chebyshev polynomial
The degree-3 Chebyshev polynomial is
\[
T_3(x)=4x^3-3x.
\]
It satisfies
\[
|T_3(x)|\le 1 \qquad (x\in[-1,1]),
\]
and among monic cubics, the minimiser is obtained by scaling \(T_3\) so its leading coefficient becomes \(1\).

Since \(T_3(x)\) has leading term \(4x^3\), divide by \(4\):
\[
p(x)=\frac14 T_3(x)=x^3-\frac34 x.
\]
So this corresponds to
\[
a=0,\qquad b=-\frac34,\qquad c=0.
\]

## 2. Why this is optimal
For this polynomial,
\[
p(x)=x^3-\frac34x.
\]
Then
\[
|p(x)|=\frac14 |T_3(x)|\le \frac14 \quad \text{for all }x\in[-1,1].
\]
So the maximum absolute value is at most \(1/4\), and in fact equals \(1/4\), since at
\[
x=1,\ -\frac12,\ -1,\ \frac12
\]
the values alternate:
\[
p(1)=\frac14,\quad p\!\left(\frac12\right)=-\frac14,\quad p\!\left(-\frac12\right)=\frac14,\quad p(-1)=-\frac14.
\]
This equioscillation is the characteristic minimax behavior, so no monic cubic can do better.

Hence the required polynomial is
\[
\boxed{x^3-\frac34 x }.
\]

Final answer: \(\displaystyle x^3-\frac34x\)
